perturbation.original_text: Customer will comply with all international, federal, state and local laws, rules, and regulations of any governmental or regulating authority ("Law") that are applicable to its business and use of the Service.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer will endeavor to comply with all international, federal, state and local laws, rules, and regulations of any governmental or regulating authority ("Law") that are applicable to its business and use of the Service.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "will comply" with "will endeavor to comply" weakens the obligation to follow the law. This introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance, which contradicts the expected standard of strict adherence to legal requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Duty Clause, Occupational Safety and Health Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The General Duty Clause requires employers to furnish a place of employment free from recognized hazards. Changing 'will comply' to 'will endeavor to comply' suggests a weaker commitment to safety regulations, potentially violating this duty.

Duties of employers and employees (a) Each employer- (1) shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees; (2) shall comply with occupational safety and health standards promulgated under this chapter. (b) Each employee shall comply with occupational safety and health standards and all rules, regulations, and orders issued pursuant to this chapter wh
perturbation.justification: The original text states "Customer will comply," which is a strict obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by saying "Customer will endeavor to comply." This creates a contradiction because endeavoring to comply implies an effort, but not necessarily adherence, to the law. Snippet 2, 29 USC 654, states that "Each employer shall comply with occupational safety and health standards promulgated under this chapter." The modified text's weaker commitment could be interpreted as failing to meet the 'shall comply' standard, particularly regarding providing a safe workplace as described in 29 USC 654 (a)(1), creating a potential violation.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party hereby irrevocably submits for all disputes to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of the state and federal courts located in New York, New York.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party hereby submits for all disputes to the jurisdiction and venue of any court the other party deems convenient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing from 'exclusive jurisdiction and venue of the state and federal courts located in New York, New York' to 'jurisdiction and venue of any court the other party deems convenient' eliminates the certainty of venue and allows for forum shopping, which can conflict with jurisdictional rules and fairness principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: This federal statute establishes rules for determining proper venue in federal courts. The modified contract language attempts to override these statutory venue provisions, potentially leading to disputes over proper venue and contradicting established legal procedures.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows venue in 'any court the other party deems convenient', directly contradicting the original text's specification of 'exclusive jurisdiction and venue of the state and federal courts located in New York, New York.' This introduces uncertainty and the potential for forum shopping, which undermines the established venue rules. The scraped law snippets, particularly 28 U.S. Code § 1391, establish rules for determining proper venue in federal courts. The changed contract language attempts to override these statutory venue provisions, potentially leading to disputes over proper venue and contradicting established legal procedures.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will protect the Confidential Information, for one (1) year from the date of disclosure, by using at least the same degree of care as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent unauthorized use, disclosure or publication.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will try to protect the Confidential Information, for one (1) year from the date of disclosure, by using a degree of care it deems appropriate, to prevent unauthorized use, disclosure or publication.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "will protect...by using at least the same degree of care as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care" with "will try to protect...by using a degree of care it deems appropriate" significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. It introduces subjectivity and reduces the standard of care, which conflicts with typical confidentiality agreement standards.
perturbation.law_citation: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings to obtain redress for misappropriation of a trade secret
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. The modification weakens the protection of Confidential Information, which could be construed as failing to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, potentially hindering a claim under the DTSA if misappropriation occurs.

perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text specifies "will protect...by using at least the same degree of care as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care," setting a clear standard. The changed text, "will try to protect...by using a degree of care it deems appropriate," introduces subjectivity and reduces the standard of care. The Law Explanation suggests this could hinder a claim under the DTSA (Defend Trade Secrets Act) if misappropriation occurs. The change falls under 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the undefined 'degree of care it deems appropriate' deviates from the standard of care usually implemented when protecting trade secrets and confidential information.
